alerts.title: Iran shoots down U.S. military helicopter near Strait of Hormuz; Trump demands response
alerts.summary: Iran shot down a U.S. military helicopter over the Strait of Hormuz on June 9, per multiple tier-1 outlets (NPR, BBC, CNBC, Bloomberg). Trump confirmed the incident and stated the U.S. 'must' respond, escalating tensions after the June 8 Iran-Israel missile exchange. This marks direct U.S.-Iran kinetic engagement, a new threshold beyond the proxy dynamics of the prior ceasefire period.

alerts.title: Iran enrichment reaches 60% U-235; nuclear breakout window shrinks to 1-2 weeks
alerts.summary: Iran's uranium enrichment confirmed at 60% U-235 with estimated breakout time of 1-2 weeks, per global_metrics nuclear feed. Five concurrent headlines (Reuters, Jerusalem Post, Al Jazeera, CSIS, World Nuclear Association) document coordinated IAEA and U.S. pressure campaign. Concurrent with active missile exchange, this represents dual escalation—kinetic and nuclear.

alerts.title: Israel strikes Tyre in Lebanon; eight killed as Hezbollah-Israel border escalates
alerts.summary: Israeli airstrikes on Tyre, Lebanon killed eight civilians on June 9 and prompted evacuation orders for the Christian quarter, per The Guardian and Axios. Incident follows Hezbollah attack on Israel and represents spillover of Iran-Israel tensions into Lebanon theater. Risk of Hezbollah retaliation and broader regional conflagration elevated.

alerts.title: U.S. carrier strike groups and amphibious assets maintain critical zone deployments amid escalation
alerts.summary: USS Dwight D. Eisenhower, USS Bataan, and three destroyers (Carney, Mason, Laboon) remain deployed in Red Sea; USS Abraham Lincoln in Arabian Sea; USS Florida submarine in Persian Gulf per assets feed (June 9). Concurrent with active U.S.-Iran kinetic exchange and threat level 9 (CRITICAL), naval posture indicates preparation for sustained regional conflict or rapid strike capability.
alerts.region: Red Sea, Arabian Sea, Persian Gulf

alerts.title: Global aviation costs spike as Hormuz blockade and conflict redirect air routes; fuel costs double year-over-year
alerts.summary: Rybar Telegram post (~116K views, June 9) reports U.S. airlines burned ~$6.5 billion on fuel in April alone due to Hormuz transit restrictions and conflict surcharges. International Air Transport Association sharply cut profit forecasts. Oil prices now at $92.46/bbl (up from baseline), and aviation jet fuel costs roughly doubled vs. prior year, cascading economic impact across supply chains.

analysis_note: U.S.-Iran direct kinetic escalation (helicopter shootdown), nuclear breakout threshold collapse (60% enrichment, 1-2 week breakout), and spillover into Lebanon signal imminent multi-front regional war risk despite ceasefire rhetoric; economic shock (oil, aviation costs) already transmitting globally.
